conclusion: The lease-provider evidence is strongest around Strategic Enablement Partner and Mobility Ecosystem Orchestrator roles. The most frequent product types are vehicle-access and finance constructs that let a MaaS proposition source, fund and keep vehicles operational, especially full-maintenance lease/rental, operating lease/rental, maintenance management, fleet management, vehicle finance/funding and commercial vehicle leasing/rental.

Mobility Marketplace and Customer Aggregation Platform signals are present but less dominant. They appear mainly through vehicle choice, preconfigured offers, subscription/flexible monthly leasing, owner-operator enablement and single-vehicle-to-fleet scalability rather than through broad consumer marketplace propositions.

Digital Origination Platform is the lightest category in the current evidence base. Online onboarding and digital offer selection appear in a small number of records, so a MaaS platform discussion should validate API readiness, digital application flows, quote-to-contract integration and data-sharing maturity directly with providers.

Product-vs-feature ambiguity is material: maintenance management, fleet management, procurement, roadside support, tyre management, telematics and fuel management can be sold as services in their own right or embedded as features of full-maintenance/fleet leases. The report therefore treats them as both where the current evidence supports market-level productization.

key_feature_implications_for_maas_partne.why_it_matters: Protects vehicle uptime and roadworthiness; often determines whether leased assets can support reliable mobility operations.
Provides flexible vehicle access without ownership, useful for MaaS pilots, fleet expansion and asset-light mobility propositions.
Bundles access and lifecycle cost control, reducing operational complexity for MaaS partners.
Lease duration, mileage and utilisation assumptions must fit MaaS demand patterns rather than conventional corporate fleet usage.
Indicates whether the provider can coordinate multi-vehicle operations beyond finance.
Determines capital-light scaling options and risk appetite for MaaS vehicle supply.
Affects vehicle suitability, availability, pricing and ability to configure mobility-specific fleets.
Critical for service continuity and customer experience when vehicles fail in operation.
Needed for utilisation visibility, operational control, reporting and potential platform integration.
Supports operating-cost visibility and can become a data feed into MaaS economics.

caveats_and_assumptions: No new providers, products or features were invented; all entries derive from current products, functionalities and capability dictionary files.
Frequency counts are evidence-occurrence counts from the dataset, not market share, customer penetration, revenue or provider capability depth.
Some standalone product/service interpretations require provider validation because public pages often bundle several services under fleet solutions.
Only the five approved MaaS categories are used in the generated analysis.
